## [00:00 - 03:00]

So now let's talk about phase one of graduation testing, which is all about finding audiences that work. So by this stage, depending on how you've taken the path through the training, you would have gone through the 5W avatar to really understand who your ideal customer is and the different angles that you can test your ad through as well. So to give you an example, if I'm targeting dog owners with a bereavement print on demand product, then if I'm targeting dog owners and picking the angle of loss as being the big emotional angle, then for example, if I find an interest that's related to the breed of a particular dog and use an image of that breed in the ad, that's going to connect far better than just going after generic dog lovers with a generic image. So that's kind of how the 5W avatar is helping us to build our audiences out. And the same kind of then applies with our ads. So I'm hoping you've been through the 3N ads formula and gone through creating your perfect ad as well. Because with this, once we know our audiences, we can construct the right message and offer for them as well. So using the principles of ADA, attraction, interest, desire and action, we want to bring that into our ads. And at the testing phase, we want to see which of those are working and kind of what's working best for us. So in this video, I want to kind of show you how we set up our phase one testing. So let me just bring my phase one up. So what we've done here is we've selected, and this is a fairly new ad account. So we've selected here the audiences that we want to test in our phase one. So we've got a 1% lookalike and some interests in a few campaigns that haven't launched yet. Now when I'm testing audiences, I do like to start with my kind of lower percentage lookalike. So I know some people want to test all of them at once. So test a 1% or 5% and a 10% or spread them out and say one, two, three, four, five, et cetera. There's a high chance that if your 1% doesn't work, then your 5% and 10% won't work, but it's not always the case. What I then like to do is I test my 1% first, especially at the audience stage to kind of test if I've got existing ads. I want to test with my best ads. If the 1% works, then I'll quickly go and test the rest of the lookalikes. If the 1% doesn't work, then I'll make a judgment call. Now, if it's a 1% lookalike of an audience that I feel should be really good. So for example, if I'm doing a 1% lookalike of purchases and I've got at least 500, if not 1000 people in my custom audience and that 1% doesn't work, then that kind of question puts into question

## [03:00 - 06:00]

whether I found the right ad or maybe the 1% is for whatever reason, just not working. I might then test a 3% or a 2% or a five, especially if I feel like the core audience should be good. Now, at this stage, when I'm kind of creating my campaigns, one thing to bear in mind as I'm using CBO is I set up a campaign for my different audience type. So for this particular account, we're only testing interests and lookalikes. I keep them separate. So when I'm doing my testing, I keep my lookalikes separate from my interests and if I'm doing broad, I keep that separate as well. So to give you an idea, if I click down to this interest group, you can see the different interests that I'm targeting here and I keep the interests separate. The reason I prefer to do that is if I find, for example, let's say here, for example, motorcycle club works well with a particular type of ad, it doesn't mean that this interest group here is gonna also perform the same. So this one here, we're actually doing an intersect of audiences based on trying to find people that have more money. So therefore someone that is a fan of Mercedes-Benz for this particular test and is into DIY. This one here is top zip codes. Better Homes and Gardens Magazine, for example. So we're trying to find intersects. Now I wouldn't necessarily bundle all of these interests into one ad set because then I don't know which ad is best performing. So we've split them out. So these are the first set of interests that we're testing. We prefer to test five at a time. Now here we've got, so I don't think this is running right now. So this one we've been through the testing phase. We've tested these five ad sets and we've decided which ones to graduate and which are not. And in this case, we were looking at both cost per initiate checkout and cost per to cart as our success metric because we're optimizing for initiate checkout. So if you've been through the account calibration part of the training, which was related to this ad account, you'll know that we selected initiate checkout for our testing purposes because we wanted to maximize the insights for the least amount of spend because on this particular ad account, we were limited on our test budget. So we're optimizing for initiate checkout and I'm looking at what kind of add to cart rate and initiate checkout costs I'm getting. ## [06:00 - 09:02]

So my kind of setup here at the campaign level, we leave the settings fairly basic. There's no manual bid or anything like that. We just set the budget in play here. My budget preference is to set a budget of $20 per ad set. Generally doing five ad sets means you can do $100 each and then if I look at the setup here, you can see what we're actually doing in terms of the audiences. So here we're excluding any audiences that are being used in re-targeting, for example. It's all US lowest cost settings, et cetera. If I go back to my KPIs. So now if I look at one of the ad sets to give you an idea of what we have and we can have a look at the setup. So at the testing phase, there's a few things that we like to put in place and I'll describe them here. So first of all, we pick the pixel events based on what we got from our calibration testing. What I will tell you right now is we are now optimizing for the purchase pixel. Number one reason is we've generated more profitability. So we're investing that back into validating our tests with our ideal purchase pixel. And also the purchase pixel has picked up a lot more data from our ideal customer as well. So those two things have come together. So we're now using the purchase pixel. Generally for testing, we always use one day click. The reason is your test budgets are small and you're changing ads frequently, you're testing different interests, et cetera. You're not looking for any kind of sustainability over a period of time. And secondly, if one of my preferences with CBO in particular is because CBO manages budgets on a daily basis for you, one day click gives it flexibility of finding people that are in market in that day to take that conversion for you. Now, I would use seven days if my average order value is higher potentially and mainly in my kind of prospecting and scaling campaigns, but for testing, we generally just focus on one day click. So now if I go down and I look at the ad spend limits, what we try and do is preserve the budget and use it fairly across the ad set. So because this is a testing phase, we want to set the ad set spend limits at $20. So as I said previously, we wanna set $20 per ad set. We've got five ad set and therefore the campaign budget is $100, but that's based on a number of things. Number one is if you've got 10 ad sets here, which you actually can do, then you'd want $200, $20 per ad set. Or if you want to test a bit faster, you might set that at $30, $40 a day times five equals,

## [09:02 - 12:04]

you know, whatever, if you're doing $50 a day times five is $250. So just balance that out and make sure you've got that in place. So the ad set spend limits are really in place so that we can make sure that the spend is fairly placed across the different ad sets. When I'm doing my prospecting and scaling with CBO, I don't like to put spend limits in place because I want Facebook to make those spend decisions. Now, for testing, I don't want Facebook to make those spend decisions. And that's why I put these controls in place. Then, you know, you can see my excluded audiences, purchasers and kind of warm target audiences as well. This is just a general US campaign. And let's just scroll down. There's my kind of age and gender. So for this particular test, we're testing engaged shoppers and people that are into home improvements because these are engaged shoppers, are people that are, have likely bought in the last 30 days. So it's the behavior where people have clicked on the shop now button and combined with optimizing for your event are people that are in market and potentially likely to execute on your target pixel as well. Now, when I'm doing my testing, and this is something which you need to bear in mind what kind of ads you're creating, generally we test on the Facebook platform first, especially for phase one, because I wanna know whether the audience is working. Now, if your brand is Instagram only, then you'd switch that out and just do Instagram only. And that totally makes sense. But for the most cases, when we're doing this, we start with Facebook just to test the audiences first and then we'll break out and start testing different platforms and ads and creatives. So for this purpose, we're just testing Facebook and Newsfeed only so that we can get the data in as simple a faction as possible. So that's generally the setup at the ad set level. If I close that down, then at the ad level, we generally run three ads per ad set. Now, the reason is, as I said, we are spending $20 a day for this particular ad set. It's not a lot of money. It's a really low budget spend. If we're running three ad sets here, we don't restrict Facebook as to which ad gets the budget and Facebook will kind of try to spend it across each. But what will happen is if we have too many ads running, it's gonna take too long to get results. So if my budget is $20 a day at the ad set level, then you divide that by three and that's roughly $6 per ad. It's not a lot. If I have one ad running, then $20 goes into one ad, that's okay. But I want to also test more. So in this case, three is probably the max for $20.

## [12:04 - 15:05]

If you're spending more like $50 a day, then three is probably a good number. If you're spending $100 a day on your testing, then you can go for five ads, for example. So that's how I'm kind of flexing the budget to ad set to ad ratio. $20 is bare minimum. So $20 per ad set with three ads is already a stretch. It just takes me longer to get results than it would otherwise. So that's kind of how I would look at how I'm pitching my ads to make sure I'm getting enough data. Like that's one of the most important things is making sure you've got enough data going through. ## [15:05 - 18:06]

or I know that work and now start iterating on the ad. So for example, if this audience here engaged plus home improvement gave me a $16 cost-per-initiate checkout and this ad showed potential, for my phase two, I'm gonna now create variables around different parts of this ad. So as you can see from the naming convention, there's a few different things at play. We've got two landing pages. This had landing page two. We've got multiple copy variants. So this was copy three and we've got multiple videos and this particular test, the only variable was the copy. So we know that copy three works, copy two and one did not work. So the next phase that we go into phase two says, let's keep copy three, let's try different videos, let's try different static images, et cetera. And that's how we go through and systematically test and squeeze performance. And what would happen is for this ad set, we would get stable results, we would get better insights into which particular format and style of ad works before we start to move into prospecting and scaling. So by the time we move it into our main campaigns, we've tested it thoroughly. This is something you cannot do if you're simply just pushing test ads into your main campaigns as many people recommend because you can't do this level of dissection to find exactly what's working. So we know copy three works, that's great. We wanna go further. Maybe we test landing page one again versus landing page two. Maybe we test video one, two, three again. So there's many ways we can squeeze out performance and maybe we end up with three or four ads that actually work well. Now for this one audience, we've got three or four ads that we can run. We run two or three live and we have one as a backup. And that's how we create this kind of whole conveyor belt factory system for testing audiences and finding ads that worked, that work for us. So this is phase one of testing. Phase one, just to reiterate, what we're doing is to try and find audiences that work by matching the ads that work as well. So just bear in mind when you're looking at your stats, you're not just looking at the top level at which ad sets are performing well, but you need to go down to the ad level because an ad set does not exist without an ad and an ad cannot exist without an ad set. So it's a pairing system. You need to look for those pairs and make sure that you have some traction. Now, one of the questions I hear is, if I test the ad sets and the ads don't work, what do I do with the ad set? I would continue to test more angles. Now, we would generally test maybe 10, maybe even 15 ads before we give up on an audience, especially if it's a lookalike.

## [18:06 - 20:44]

For interest, we would take a judgment on it, but we would test to see whether, you know, multiple angles in ad formats can work for an audience before absolutely giving up on it. So if I go into this lookalike audience, let's find an example. So this one here, a customer list January 2020. If I go into that, the ads that have run, there's not really good performance here. So for this one, for example, the audience in theory should work. So these are recent purchases, for example, for this particular campaign. If the ad set isn't working, I would go and now introduce a new batch of ads. So if batch one didn't work, which were these standard ads here, which we've been testing across our different ad sets, I'd then introduce batch two, which is another three ads, then batch three, which is another three ads, and really test to see if it's working or not. Now, the other question I often get is, also around lookalikes, how do you, I kind of group them for my testing, I would group them based on the kind of percentages. So if I'm running my audience testing here, I would run the 1% lookalikes, the 2% et cetera. They can run a separate campaigns, especially if you've got different types of lookalikes. So one way of looking at it is, if you've got a 1% lookalike campaign, then in that campaign, you've got different types of seed audiences for your lookalikes. So here we've got, let's have a look. So 180 days. So people that have bought more than two units over 180 days, we've got people that have engaged, purchases, customers. So these are all different 1% audiences. Then I would create a separate 2% campaign for all my different 2% et cetera. That decision comes down, especially at the testing phase, as to how you like to organize yourself. Some people would just create a lookalike campaign and test all of their ones and twos et cetera. I prefer a bit more order. So I create my 1% here, 2% here. If I'm testing a broader lookalike, so for example, 1% to 5% is one big audience. I create that in a separate campaign as well. So my testing is more organizational based, as opposed to anything to do with optimization, which we'll cover off in the prospecting stage, but that just helps me to manage what I am looking at for my audiences and helps that testing process to kind of help me understand the analysis as well.
